data.title: These Altcoins Bleed Out as Bitcoin (BTC) Struggles at $84K (Weekend Watch)
data.description:
Bitcoin’s relatively positive business week ended on the wrong foot as the asset erased all gains and plunged below $84,000.

The altcoins are also struggling on a daily scale, with ETH dropping beneath $1,900, while XRP is well under $2.15.BTC Slips to $84K

It all seemed to be going well for the primary cryptocurrency, whose price started to gain traction last Sunday and peaked on Monday. Within that 24-hour period, the asset jumped by several grand andmarkeda multi-week high of just under $89,000. The ETF inflows hadchangedfor the better, and the overall sentiment had improved.

However, BTC couldn’t break through that resistance and started to consolidate in the next few days, trading mostly in a tight range between $86,000 and $88,000. On Friday, though, the lower boundary gave in and bitcoin slumped beneath it.

It kept going south and dropped to $83,500 for the first time since last Friday. Thus, BTClostall gains made during the week. As of now, it struggles below $84,000 once again, while its market cap has plunged to $1.665 trillion on CG. The silver lining is its growing dominance over the alts, which has spiked to 59.1% as most have bled out heavily.Alts Turn Red

As mentioned above, most altcoins have posted notable price declines lately. Ethereum, which challenged $2,100 earlier this week, is below $1,900 now after another drop in the past 24 hours. Ripple’s native token has lost another 4% of value despite the positive news for the company this week. As a result, XRP sits beneath $2.15.

More daily losses come from the likes of BNB, SOL, DOGE, ADA, LINK, TON, SUI, and many others. In fact, the last two have lost over 6% of value in the past day alone.

With most other alts in a similar state, it’s no wonder that the total crypto market cap has dropped to $2.810 trillion – almost $200 billion lower than this week’s peak.

data.title: US warns French companies they must comply with Trump's diversity ban
data.description:
The Trump administration has written to French companies holding U.S. government contracts ordering them to comply with an executive order banning diversity, equity and inclusion programmes, two people familiar with the matter said.

The companies have been told to confirm their compliance in a separate questionnaire entitled "Certification Regarding Compliance With Applicable Federal Anti-Discrimination Law." Reuters has seen a copy of the questionnaire.

The documents will raise concerns in European boardrooms that the Trump administration is widening its fight against DEI overseas, at a time U.S. President Donald Trump's actions on tariffs and security ties have upended trans-Atlantic relations.

French business daily Les Echos, which first reported the existence of the letter late on Friday, said the missive had been sent out to firms by the U.S. embassy in Paris.

"We inform you that Executive Order 14173, Ending Illegal Discrimination and Restoring Merit-based Opportunities, signed by President Trump, applies to all suppliers and service providers of the U.S. Government, regardless of their nationality and the country in which they operate," reads the letter, according to a copy that French newspaper Le Figaro published on its website.

"We would be grateful if you could complete and sign the document in English within five days and return it to us by email. If you do not agree to sign this document, we would appreciate if you could provide detailed reasons, which we will forward to our legal services," the letter added, with reference to the certification seen by Reuters.

An embassy spokesman did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

An official close to French Finance Minister Eric Lombard said the matter would be taken up with the U.S. government.

"This practice reflects the values of the new U.S. government. They are not the same as ours. The minister will remind his counterparts in the U.S. government of this," the official said.

Reuters could not immediately identify which companies had received the letter. Defence and infrastructure companies were among those that risked exposure, media reported.

It was not immediately clear if similar letters and questionnaires had been sent to foreign companies in other European countries.

Trump has sought to eradicate DEI initiatives, which he and other critics say are discriminatory, from the government and private sector.

data.title: How the End of the 'American Exceptionalism' Trade Is Rippling Around the Globe
data.description:
By Chelsey Dulaney

What do priests in New Zealand, Taiwanese life insurers and the oil-rich nation of Norway have in common? They have all been riding high on booming U.S. markets — and are now vulnerable to a reversal in fortunes.

The superior performance of U.S. investments over the past decade-plus has been a giant magnet for the world's money. Foreign investors own almost 20% of all U.S. equities compared with 7% at the start of this century, Goldman Sachs data shows. The bets generated years of windfall profits, making up for dreary returns on offer in many overseas markets.

Now, foreigners are racking up losses as the so-called American exceptionalism trade sputters. The Trump administration's tariff whiplash, doubts about the U.S. artificial-intelligence trade, and recession fears have rattled U.S. markets this year, even as European ones have rallied.

The selloff could prove temporary. But it is stoking concern that if U.S. markets really were to crater, the collateral damage would be profound.

"You've got an enormous global concentration in U.S. financial markets," said Brad Setser, senior fellow at the Council on Foreign Relations and a former U.S. Treasury Department official. "Investors are taking an increasingly big risk."

Overseas investors are more vulnerable to U.S. market turbulence because of their exposure to the dollar. The S&P 500 is down about 9% from its February peak, but eurozone investors have lost about 13% due to the dollar's slide, according to FactSet data.

"Looking back in history at episodes when concentration was so high, it always ends," said Torsten Slok, chief economist at Apollo Global Management. "The finance textbook is clear: You should simply not allow your U.S. share to grow so big."

The large presence of the U.S. in global portfolios is a byproduct of years of measly returns abroad — and huge success in America, thanks to fast economic growth, thick profit margins, and innovative tech companies such as Apple and Nvidia. The U.K.'s FTSE 100 index is up less than 30% over the past decade. The S&P 500, even after this year's selloff, is up more than 170%.

The rise of passive investing, in which funds track a broad market index, also plays a role. As U.S. markets have grown so has their share in indexes, creating built-in demand for American assets.

U.S. stocks make up 72% of MSCI's index of international stocks, a benchmark for many global portfolios. That is up from about 47% in 2008. American debt's share in global bond benchmarks also has shot higher.

In one of London's poshest areas, the Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea, the local government's pension fund has enough money to pay out all its obligations two times over thanks largely to U.S. stocks.

In New Zealand, the pension fund for Anglican Church clergy notched a 27% increase on its international stock portfolio in its last fiscal year. Norway's $1.8 trillion oil-wealth fund posted its largest-ever profit thanks to "massive gains" on its tech portfolio, according to Nicolai Tangen, chief executive of Norges Bank Investment Management, which operates the fund.

"We are very happy shareholders in great American companies. For the last 10 years, the U.S. has compounded at twice the rate of Europe," Tangen said.

He and others have concerns, however, especially about the market's reliance on U.S. tech. Previous U.S. financial-market downturns were global events. The 2008-09 subprime mortgage crisis ricocheted across the global financial system, and the dot-com crash in the early 2000s fueled recessions in economies such as Taiwan's that had built up large tech sectors.

Norway's fund has trimmed U.S. tech holdings, but its overall exposure to the U.S. has been rising because of a government decision to put more money in America. U.S. assets made up 53% of the fund's investments last year, up from 32% a decade earlier. It can deviate only slightly from its benchmarks, which are largely dominated by U.S. assets.

The fund's latest stress-test scenario showed it could lose 18% of its value if the AI boom turns to bust. The wealth fund contributes about 20% of Norway's government budget and helps pay for its generous social-welfare programs.

Individual investors have piled in, too. Trading apps have opened up U.S. markets to investors in Europe, where people traditionally stashed savings in cash, bank accounts and insurance policies. The most popular stocks on European trading app Trade Republic last year were Nvidia, Apple and Amazon.

Omer Martinet, 29 years old, of Bordeaux, France, started investing in 2020 and now holds 70% of his stock portfolio in U.S. companies.

"I compared the performance of French companies to U.S. companies, and the difference was astonishing," he said.

Martinet, who is up overall on his investments, said he had lost "tens of thousands of euros" this year because of the U.S. market selloff. If they don't recover, he might have to delay his plan to buy an apartment. But he isn't giving up on his U.S. investments.

"I think the U.S. economy will remain, for at least this century, the main economy in the world," he said.

The dollar has weakened this year. A further selloff is a threat to overseas investors, who owned more than $14 trillion in U.S. bonds at the end of 2024, according to Federal Reserve data. Many only partially shield themselves against currency swings, a practice known as hedging.

Taiwanese life insurers are among the most exposed, said Setser of the Council on Foreign Relations. They own more than $700 billion in foreign bonds, he said, mainly U.S. dollar debt. Most of their liabilities are in Taiwan dollars. About 40% of this currency mismatch isn't hedged.

Many foreign investors say the U.S. still has more to offer than much of the rest of the world. Australia's largest pension fund, AustralianSuper, has about a third of its $230 billion in assets in the U.S. and is expanding its New York office. John Normand, head of investment strategy, expects the U.S. market turbulence will be temporary.

"The U.S. is first in the league table for interesting investment opportunities that meet all of our criteria: scale, scope, cyclical strength, structural strength," Normand said. "The U.S. is going to have certain advantages for a while."

Write to Chelsey Dulaney at chelsey.dulaney@wsj.com

data.title: NAYG lawsuit against Galaxy was ‘lawfare, pure and simple' — Scaramucci
data.description:
The New York State Attorney General’s (NAYG) recent legal action against Galaxy Digital over its promotional ties to the now-collapsed cryptocurrency Terra (LUNA) was unfair and an abuse of the legal system, says SkyBridge Capital and founder Anthony Scaramucci.

“It’s LAWFARE, pure and simple due to an obscure but dangerously powerful New York law known as the Martin Act,” Scaramucci said in a March 28 X post.

Martin Law can “open the door for abuse”

“The law has no need to prove intent, creating a low standard of proof that can open the door for abuse like this. It shouldn’t exist,” he said.

New York’s Martin Act is one of the US's strictest anti-fraud and securities laws, allowing prosecutors the power to pursue financial fraud cases without needing to prove intent. The NAYG alleged that Galaxy Digital violated the Martin Act over its alleged promotion of Terra, with Galaxy Digital agreeing to a $200 million settlement.

According to NAYG documents filed on March 24, Galaxy Digital acquired 18.5 million LUNA tokens at a 30% discount in October 2020, then promoted them before selling them without abiding by disclosure rules. 

Scaramucci reiterated that Galaxy CEO Michael Novogratz was under the impression everything he was saying about Luna was true, as he had been deceived by Terraform Labs and its former CEO, Do Kwon.

Meanwhile, MoonPay president of enterprise, Keith Grossman, said he had never heard of the Martin Act and had to look it up using AI chatbot ChatGPT.

“It is so broad and essentially is the essence of lawfare,” Grossman said. “Sorry you got caught in the crosshairs of it, Mike,” he added.

The filing alleged that Galaxy helped a “little-known” token, referring to LUNA, increase its market price from $0.31 in October 2020 to $119.18 in April 2022 while “profiting in the hundreds of millions of dollars.”

Asset manager and investor Anthony Pompliano said he isn’t familiar with the details of the lawsuit but vouched for Novogratz, calling him a “good man” who has devoted a lot of time and money to helping others.

The Terra collapse is one of the crypto industry’s most infamous failures. In March 2024, SEC attorney Devon Staren said in the US District Court for the Southern District of New York that Terra was a “house of cards” that collapsed for investors in 2022.

REP. ANNA PAULINA LUNA (R-FL) IS INTERVIEWED ON FOX NEWS` JESSE

WATTERS PRIMETIME

MARCH 28, 2025

SPEAKERS:

REP. ANNA PAULINA LUNA (R-FL)

JESSE WATTERS, FOX NEWS HOST

JESSE WATTERS, FOX NEWS HOST: Congresswoman Anna Paulina Luna joins me now.

You are so young, Congresswoman, that I'm going to ask you how old you are. How old are you?

REP. ANNA PAULINA LUNA (R-FL): I'm 35.

WATTERS: All right, so you're incredibly young. You're 35 and you see this new generation coming up.

Do you understand how important this is? The President does.

LUNA: Yes, most certainly. In fact, Jesse, it's interesting that you're bringing that up. I'm currently working on something that would allow new parents, and specifically female members of Congress to vote by proxy when they're recovering from childbirth.

So look, we need a younger Congress. We are currently seeing right now that the American people want to have young representation, we have to carry the torch, and it means bringing some of these institutional changes that I believe will be better for the American people in the long run.

WATTERS: And if you're not growing, you're dying, and that's why Greenland is necessary. Do you have colleagues in the House that are against taking Greenland?

LUNA: No, I don't think so. Well, actually, I take that back. I think that there's been some murmurs of people that think that it might just be messaging, but I would actually argue, from a strategic standpoint, Jesse, that that would actually be a great location for us, not to mention, from what I gather, the people of Greenland are very much supportive of becoming potentially a U.S. territory.

And so I'm supportive of it. Why would we not want to expand our message and really have people that are for the America First and pro-American ideology?

WATTERS: When we talk tariffs and trade and the President talks about buying American, why is that important? People don't usually get it.

LUNA: Well as of right now, specifically in the 70s, when big corporations started sending industry overseas, what ultimately ended up happening is it actually hurt us back home. A lot of jobs ended up going down. We obviously lost our steel industry. And so when the President talks about reciprocal tariffs, it's important for people to realize that's actually a very good negotiating tool as well.

What ends up happening is those countries no longer want to, for example, like China, put a hundred percent tariff on product. If we are charging them, then the same. And so it actually will help, in the long run, to not just bring costs down for the American people, but also manufacturing back to the United States.

I've actually been a huge advocate for the President's perspective on this idea.

WATTERS: Good, and we're hearing a rumor there might be a hearing about the JFK situation. Can you tell us a little bit about that?

LUNA: Yes, so we actually have our hearing coming up on April 1st. That is not April Fool's Day. That's when the hearing is going to be and we actually just confirmed today that the director, Oliver Stone will be testifying at that hearing, as well as Jeff Morley and a few other people.

You know, there's been some incredible breakthrough on these declassified documents, and kudos to President Trump for following through on his campaign promise to fully declassify but look, Jesse, we need to ensure that the American people deserve the truth, get down to the facts of what actually happened.

And I do believe that the Warren Commission never did the American people the facts. And so we have those. We're excited to present them to the American people, and there's more to come on that.

WATTERS: Jeff Morley, who was on my show, who's going to be testifying to your committee next week, he said, because of these new releases, it's conclusive that the CIA was running Oswald, running him. And he also said that he doesn't think all of the documents have been released. Is that your understanding, too?

LUNA: Yes, so we're actually in the process of tracking down two specific documents and also, too, Jesse, I would like to actually tell the American people it was made aware to me this evening that NBC actually has a video that's never been seen before. We're actually going to be a sending a letter requesting that from NBC, because it allegedly shows Oswald near the vehicle when the assassination took place, which means that he couldn't have been the shooter.

So again, we're tracking down all this information, but look, there's even a CIA document that came out that Mr. Morley pointed out, that actually said that the CIA never bought the lone gunman theory. And so I think the American people had an inclination as to what we are saying, but we never had the hard evidence until now.

And so it's important to note that in a free and fair society, how could you operate or have an agency operating in the shadows? And so kudos to President Trump, also director Ratcliffe and Tulsi Gabbard for pushing for this transparency. It is going to be generational changing that they've done this, and we hope to bring forward legislation too, to ensure that this never happens again for future generations to come.

WATTERS: You're saying NBC has been keeping this tape of Oswald under wraps?

LUNA: Correct. In fact, Director Stone actually told us that he was showed this tape, that it was a secondary copy, and that he said that this could blow open the entire JFK investigation.

What I will also tell you though, Jesse, is he said that NBC has been very, very much so guarding this tape. And so I believe that that tape belongs to the American people. We are going to be sending a letter asking for that tape, and I would encourage everyone to ask NBC to release that tape to the public. It's important, not just for our investigations, but so the American people know the truth as to what happened with John F. Kennedy.

WATTERS: All right, well, I think they will release it, because I believe the President will probably put it on Truth Social pretty soon.

All right, Congresswoman Anna Paulina Luna —

LUNA: Well, hopefully Fox promotes it. Yes.

WATTERS: There you go. Thank you so much.

LUNA: Thank you.

WATTERS: Have a great weekend.

LUNA: You, too.

END

SEN. BERNIE MORENO (R-OH) IS INTERVIEWED ON FOX NEWS` HANNITY

MARCH 28, 2025

SPEAKERS:

SEN. BERNIE MORENO (R-OH)

JASON CHAFFETZ, FOX NEWS HOST

JASON CHAFFETZ, FOX NEWS HOST: Speaking on the Trump tariffs, Ohio Senator Bernie Moreno said any short-term pain will be worth it in the long run for the auto industry. Remember, Senator Moreno used to own a very successful car dealership business, and he joins us right now.

Senator, thank you so much for joining us on "Hannity".

Explain more where you're at on these tariffs. What's this going to mean to the auto industry and for our nation.

SEN. BERNIE MORENO (R-OH): Well, first, let me just tell you that on Monday, we are introducing legislation to do what President Trump said, which is to make interest on autos purchased that are made in America deductible. So, we're going to do that on Monday. What President Trump is doing is he's saving auto workers and steel workers and the middle class in this country.

Look, for 40, 50 years, our industrial sector got gutted because of terrible trade deals. It's very simple, Jason, if you sell a car in America, you're going to make it in America. And the reality is the tariffs will hit primarily very expensive automobiles and in effect, that's a tax on the wealthy. You would think the Democrats would be in support of that. But when it comes to the cars that middle class Americans come — buy, those cars will be made right here in America and the cost is going to go down because we're going to get rid of all these electric vehicle mandates.

CHAFFETZ: Now, one of the things that President Trump is at least hinting at, at least it's being reported at, is that he's okay increasing taxes on the wealthy to cover the cost of not taxing tips. Where are you at on that?

MORENO: Yeah , 100 percent. Look, we are the party of the working class. Now, it's taken a while for us to get here. The Democrats are the party of the elites. The party of the crazies, the party of Hollywood, university professors. We're the party of the auto workers, steel workers, the people who built this country, the people who deserve to live a good life where a mom or a dad can have one full-time job, buy a home, buy a car, afford to go on vacation periodically and retire with dignity. That's what our party stands for, that's what we stand behind.

And look, with the money we're going to collect with tariffs, Jason, we're going to be able to restore Medicare, Medicaid and Social Security for generations to come for those very workers that pay into the system.

CHAFFETZ: Yeah, if you combine that with the more efficiency with what DOGE is trying to do and duplicate payments and all the other horror stories we hear about, that's how you put those things on financial footing. That's how you get them back and moving in the same direction.

But, Senator, can — can the Congress handle that? Can Congress actually keep up with the president by passing the legislation needed? And can you do one big beautiful bill in reconciliation? We've — we — look, we've stuck together. Thus far, Senator Thune is doing an amazing job. Senator Barrasso is doing a great job we have all new leadership in the United States Senate.

We've stuck together on confirmations. We've gotten the congressional review acts done. We're working hard every single day and I'm confident that we're going to get a bill that protects Medicare, Medicaid and Social Security, puts in place policies that help working Americans and absolutely show the country that the Democrats have no ideas, no leadership and no uniting principles other than they're held hostage by the extremists in their party.

And I think Mike Johnson, Speaker Johnson's also doing a great job, and he's going to get his conference across the finish line. We're going to get the work done.

CHAFFETZ: Yeah, it is true about the Democrats because all you hear is, oh, we're going to be the resistance. And then you hear no alternatives of the any viability on what they suggest should be done.

Last question, Senator. Donald Trump is giving master classes on actually how to negotiate. I guess what a lot of people don't appreciate, particularly with tariffs and working with these foreign nations, is that he is negotiating. It's — it's his tool. He — but he tells us and foreshadows what he wants as a consequence, and that is parity, he wants to make sure that there's not these massive trade deficits.

MORENO: Well, I agree with that. I think he's looking for fair and reciprocal trade, but when it comes to steel, when it comes to the auto industry, when it comes to energy, semiconductor chips, soon pharmaceuticals, I think it's not negotiation. I think this is the rules of the road, you must make your car in America or you're going to be tariffed.

We want that industrial capacity back. We have to rebuild our industrial heartland, and I think President Trump's going to do that. He's going to do it in short order. It's an imperative. He campaigned on it. I campaigned on it, and we're going to get it done.

CHAFFETZ: Yeah, it is amazing the speed and velocity of where Donald Trump is taking us and glad to hear you talk about, can't wait to — to get the details of where we're going with pharmaceuticals because that'll impact a lot of people.

Senator Moreno, thank you so much for joining us on "Hannity". Do appreciate it.

END

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

2236 GMT - xAI's acquisition of X brings the artificial intelligence startup's valuation to $80 billion, up from $50 billion as of November and more than triple its $24 billion valuation from May, according to Elon Musk. The deal, and significantly higher valuation, comes as other AI startups raise questions over the future of AI funding after last year's surging investor enthusiasm. CoreWeave, expected to have one of the biggest IPOs of the year, priced its initial public offering well below expectations and ended its first day of trading flat on Friday. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1841 GMT - Braze's latest results and plans to acquire AI-decisioning company OfferFit show it continues to beat other marketing tech companies on innovation, according to JPMorgan in a research note. On the company's analyst call, Braze executives said the deal will help Braze differentiate versus competitors by providing a wide spectrum of AI-driven optimization capabilities at various price points and service levels. Braze is "redefining the marketing tech stack and continues to solidify its position as a critical first-party data set for companies," JPMorgan says, adding the acquisition is expected to add around 2 points of revenue growth in fiscal 2026. Braze is up 3.3%. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1516 GMT - The S&P 500 still remains expensive even after declining from its peak, say analysts at BofA Securities in a research note, as they handicap the investing landscape in the U.S. and Europe. According to the analysts, the bear case for US equities includes the capex arms race between hyperscalers increasing margin risk, among other things, and growing policy uncertainty amid higher government debt. But there are still structural advantages in the U.S., the analysts say, including clean corporate--and most consumer--balance sheets; high corporate transparency; USD reserve status; energy independence; looser labor laws, and U.S. technology and innovation still the world's leader. European equities, meanwhile, have a bull case amid stimulus from the German government and defense spending by the European Union. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1350 GMT — Galaxy Digital Holdings expects to make billions on an agreement with CoreWeave, the startup that rents out access to Nvidia chips. The digital asset and blockchain company entered into a 15-year lease agreement with CoreWeave, which makes its public debut later Friday, in which it will provide 133 megawatts of data center capacity to host and support CoreWeave's artificial intelligence and high-performance computing efforts. Over the life of the agreement, Galaxy expects to generate about $4.5 billion in revenue. Benchmark's Mark Palmer in a research note says the estimated revenue from the deal likely reflects CoreWeave's commitment to fund all the capital investments required to upgrade its data centers, among other things. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1229 GMT - A Telus sale of a minority interest in its wireless tower portfolio could be a good way to help deleverage the company. While the decision isn't final, Desjardins' Jerome Dubreuil says that there shouldn't be any regulatory issues, and that it shouldn't interfere with its network-sharing agreement with BCE. "Infrastructure divestitures have the potential to help deleveraging and to unlock value," Dubreuil says. He notes that management indicated that all of the potential proceeds would go toward debt repayment.(adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1212 GMT - Braze's latest results didn't appear to show evidence of deterioration despite concerns over the health of the consumer, nor did it flag a material change in the demand environment, say UBS' Taylor McGinnis and Jeff Hickey in a research note. The customer-engagement platform logged higher revenue in F4Q, while its guidance for FY26 met expectations, the analysts say. They think that while Braze isn't commenting on the timing of a bottom, it could happen as early as 1Q. "We continue to like the set-up for Braze given company-specific tailwinds related to improving execution, lapping of tough Covid renewals and the incumbent placement opportunity," say the analysts. Braze jumps 12% premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1105 GMT - Canada's telcos may be more resilient side if tariffs escalate, and could even navigate the worst of a recession if it were to come to that. TD Cowen's Vince Valentini says in a report that telecom services are to consumers "second in importance only to food, shelter, electricity." Meaning that wireless service and internet will be a top priority to maintain, even in an economic downturn. The analyst acknowledges that telcos aren't immune, however. Revenue could be slightly weighed at the margin such as smaller package sizes for cable, lower roaming revenue due to less travel abroad, and perhaps more aggressive shopping for a better deal on wireless plans. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1858 GMT - Eli Lilly's LillyDirect expansion is a positive step as access to adequate providers remains a barrier for Alzheimer's patients, Citi analysts say in a research note. Without an early diagnosis, a patient's disease can often progress beyond the scope of approved Alzheimer's drugs like Eli Lilly's Kisunla, the analysts say. The initiative does not provide a medication dispensing option for Alzheimer's patients, though, so the onus remains on patients to take the first step to receive treatment, the analysts say, adding they maintain tempered expectations for Kisunla given a lack of treating specialists. "Neuroscience's growth will be a slow grind relative to the company's other franchises," they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1511 GMT - Novo Nordisk first-quarter earnings could miss expectations on lower-than-expected prescriptions of Ozempic and Wegovy as well as some price declines, Bank of America Securities analysts write. Growth will also be hit by tough comparables, currency impacts and possibly higher costs, the bank says. "We expect sales guide to be lowered from 16-24% at constant exchange rates to 14%-22%, with our forecasts at the bottom of the new range." BofA assumes limited Ozempic/Wegovy inflection in the second quarter, while a key unknown is if whether a second-half inflection in prescriptions happens against tougher comparables. The bank lowers its price objective to 910 Danish kroner from 1,075 kroner and keeps a buy rating. Shares trade at 475.00 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0857 GMT - EssilorLuxottica should be able to post higher sales despite uncertainty in the U.S., RBC Capital Markets analysts say. The analysts expect the Franco-Italian eyewear group to report revenue of 6.94 billion euros, up 9.5% on year in reported terms. There are some concerns around the company's exposure to the U.S. market, which accounts for 45% of its revenue, according to RBC Capital Market estimates. "Despite increasing volatility in U.S. consumer market, we think EssilorLuxottica's momentum is unlikely to be materially affected," they say. The company has a stable underlying business and mid-term growth opportunities through innovation, the analysts add. Shares are down 0.4% at 268.30 euros. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

2250 GMT - The U.S. consumer is taking a more mindful approach to beauty spending, Jefferies analysts say in a research note after meeting with Euromonitor. About 72% of consumers remain concerned about increasing everyday costs. Meanwhile, 33% of consumers plan to spend more on health and wellness, suggesting consumers continue to prioritize beauty in their budgets. Still, data shows consumers' spending is only up on certain categories, taking a more value-seeking approach in other categories. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

2159 GMT - Jefferies' latest earnings came in below Wall Street expectations, hit by a slowdown in deal activity, UBS analysts say in a research note. That environment appears to have curbed momentum for the investment bank, with shares trading down around 33% year-to-date. UBS lowers its FY25 EPS estimate on Jefferies to $3.30 from $4.50 previously and its FY26 estimate to $5.05 from $5.90 previously, reflecting lower investment banking revenue and less comp leverage as prior year recruiting costs limit expense flexibility. UBS also cuts its target price to $75 a share from $92 a share previously. Shares closed down 3.9% to $52.22. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

2009 GMT - The consumer staples sector has recorded the third-largest percent decrease in estimated earnings since the start of the first quarter after the consumer discretionary sector, and the materials sector, according to the latest FactSet earnings insight. About 92% of the S&P 500 companies in the consumer staples sector have seen a decrease in their EPS estimates during this time, with the expected earnings drop for the sector set at 7.6% from a 0.4% decline on Dec. 31. Of those companies, 14 out of 35 have recorded a decline in their EPS estimate of more than 10%, led by Estée Lauder. However, Walmart has been the largest contributor to the decrease in expected earnings for this sector since Dec. 31, according to the report. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1829 GMT - Gold futures rise 0.8% to a record $3,086.50 a troy ounce, the fourth consecutive week that gold finishes higher. Signs of sticky inflation as evidenced by the February PCE report made gold again attractive for investors. "[It's] suggesting the Fed is likely to stay on hold beyond June as they assess the tariff inflation when and where it will pop up from," Peter Cardillo of Spartan Capital Securities says. Central banks also continue to be big buyers of gold inventories. SPDR Gold shares are up 0.7% in afternoon trading. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1549 GMT - Rio Tinto investor and investment-management company Ninety One is confident that the miner's board has done the necessary work to assess the merits of collapsing its dual-listed structure and trusts its view that it poses risks to shareholders, Portfolio Manager George Cheveley told Dow Jones Newswires. "We acknowledge that the structure is not ideal but believe Rio Tinto is at an exciting strategic point and want to see the management team focused on operational delivery," he added. U.K. hedge fund Palliser has tabled resolutions calling for an independent review into the miner's structure at its annual general meetings. Ninety One Ltd. has a $120 million stake in Rio Tinto PLC--or under 0.2% of its shares--according to data from Refinitiv. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1434 GMT - The cost of insuring European bank bonds against default using credit default swaps rises due to increased concerns on the impact of tariffs ahead of the April 2 deadline, with many hitting their highest in 10 weeks or more. "European markets are on the back foot in early trade once again, with tariff fears driving risk assets lower globally," Scope Markets' Joshua Mahony says in a note. The five-year CDS on the U.K.'s HSBC 5-year CDS rises to its highest in more than five months at 55 basis points, while Germany's Deutsche Bank 5-year CDS trades at a 10-week high of 62 basis points, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. The 5-year CDS of UniCredit, NatWest, Santander and UBS also hit 10-week highs. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1356 GMT - Stocks of European banks trade lower on Friday as the market awaits the rollout of Trump's tariff agenda on April 2. The Stoxx Europe 600 Banks index sheds 1.33% in afternoon trade, after falling as much as 1.92% in earlier exchanges with Germany's Commerzbank and Austria's Raiffeisen and Bawag seeing the steepest declines. "There is some apprehension around U.S. tariff announcements next week and a general sense of unease about what that may mean for the European economies," Keefe, Bruyette & Woods analyst Andrew Stimpson says. Shares in Deutsche Bank--which earlier announced the departure of its CFO and the renewal of its CEO's contract--also fall more than 2%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1017 GMT - Irish bank shares have taken a hit as U.S. trade tariffs make headlines, given their potential impact on the country's economy, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note. The U.S. administration is eyeing the enviable budget surplus that Ireland has, partly thanks to large corporate tax receipts as well as the large trade surplus Ireland has with the U.S. in goods and pharmaceuticals in particular, analyst Andrew Stimpson writes. "We currently think it unlikely that there are widespread job losses from tariffs as Irish factories also serve the EU, not just the U.S.," he notes. AIB Group and Bank of Ireland have harsh downside scenarios in their provision forecasts, which include trade disruptions, and would take a hit of 10% and 20%, respectively, on profits, he adds. AIB is down 12% month-to-date while BoI has fallen 3%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1012 GMT - Most large European banks recorded weaker liquidity ratios in the last quarter of 2024, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. Twenty of Europe's 30 largest banks by assets recorded declines in liquidity coverage ratios in the fourth quarter, S&P Global Market Intelligence says. Nine lenders reported increases, and one had an unchanged liquidity coverage ratio, it says. Liquidity coverage ratios measure banks' strength to cope with cash outflows for 30 days. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0954 GMT - European banks are likely to issue the same or slightly fewer corporate bonds in 2025 relative to 2024, CreditSights analysts say in a note. Most European banks have met their minimum regulatory capital targets and therefore have no need to issue incremental debt, the analysts say. The level of new bond issuance can impact spread levels with higher issuance potentially causing credit spreads to widen, CreditSights says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0921 GMT - Euro investment-grade credit spreads have tightened to nearly their tightest historical levels, boosting excess returns on the assets in the year to date, CreditSights analysts say in a note. On aggregate, the excess return gains on euro investment-grade bonds are plus 73 basis points year-to-date, comprised of 51 basis points from spread tightening and 22 basis points from carry, the analysts say. Some of the notable outperforming sectors are utilities, bank surbordinate bonds, insurance subordinate bonds, transportation and autos, CreditSights says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0832 GMT - Deutsche Bank's CFO transition seems well organized and sets the stage for the next leg of the German group's strategy, which is expected to be announced along its 2025 results next year at the latest, J.P.Morgan says in a research note. DB announced the departure of its CFO James von Moltke when his contract expires in June 2026 and named Morgan Stanley's Raja Akram as his replacement. "The transition looks very well thought out with the designated CFO joining in October 2025 and overlapping with James," analysts Kian Abouhossein and Amit Ranjan write. They say the von Moltke's departure is a surprise as he and CEO Christian Sewing--whose contract was renewed until 2029--achieved a remarkable turnaround in the past five years. "The incoming CFO will have his work cut out," they note. Shares fall 2.5%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1924 GMT - Crude futures rise for a third week in a row as U.S. sanctions against Iranian and Venezuelan oil raise some near-term supply concerns, even with OPEC+ due to start unwinding some output cuts next week. "We are approaching overbought technical territory, but if there are consecutive closes above longer-term moving averages, we could be set for a higher move in Q2," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. The second quarter is expected to be tighter than originally thought, "and if there are reductions in Venezuelan or Iranian crude oil barrels on the market this would certainly be a bullish development," he says. WTI settles down 0.8% at $69.36 a barrel for a 1.6% weekly gain. Brent slips 0.5% to $73.63 and is up 2% from a week ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1729 GMT - Rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. fell by two this week to 484, or 22 fewer than a year ago, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. Wells directed at natural gas edged up by one to 103, compared with 112 a year-ago. Respondents in the Dallas Fed's 1Q survey of exploration and production and oil services firms estimated that companies need $65 a barrel oil on average to profitably drill new wells, up from $64 in the year-earlier survey. Average breakeven prices across regions ranged from $61 to $70 a barrel. The average year-end price expectation among respondents for WTI was $68 a barrel. More than half of the oil and gas services firms said they expect the impact of steel import tariffs to slightly decrease customer demand in 2025. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1420 GMT - The strength in Canada's economy at the start of the year won't alter the central bank's view that it will need to balance the deflationary impact of the tariffs due to weaker economic activity against inflationary pressures arising from higher costs with the tariffs, Alberta Central's Charles St-Arnaud says. The economist says it is clear from recent comments by Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem that at this point there is more concern over inflationary risks, which suggests that without further meaningful tariffs being imposed in the coming weeks it is likely the BoC will keep its policy rate unchanged at the April meeting. Still, St-Arnaud said that while the bank may pause the general direction for interest rates is likely lower. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1411 GMT - The Canadian economy's strong start to 2025 should keep the central bank on hold in April, strategists at TD Securities argue. Monthly GDP growth of 0.4% in January was ahead of expectations and, even with the flash estimate for growth to stall in February, leaves GDP for 1Q tracking near 2% annualized which matches the Bank of Canada's now dated projection from January, they say. The strategists note that growth will come under more pressure after tariffs take effect in March, but the latest data does leave the Canadian economy on a much stronger footing ahead of the trade shock. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1410 GMT - The latest Canadian GDP numbers likely won't mean much for the Bank of Canada's next interest rate decision, reckons Oxford Economics' Michael Davenport, who continues to expect the central bank will hold steady as it tries to balance the negative hit to demand from tariffs against upside risks to inflation. The economist says the economy got an artificial boost in January as firms pulled forward exports to the U.S. ahead of tariffs, and temporary fiscal stimulus measures continued to support demand. "January may represent the peak in economic activity as the U.S.-Canada trade war pushes Canada's economy into a recession beginning in 2Q," he says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1408 GMT - Solid Canadian GDP growth in January isn't a sign of things to come. The economy expanded a stronger-than-expected 0.4% on-month, and growth for December was revised up a tick. That, says Capital Economics' Thomas Ryan, shows the economy still had solid momentum to start the year. And the weak preliminary February estimate, showing GDP was flat, can't be blamed entirely o U.S. tariff threats since the winter weather was unseasonably harsh, he adds. Still, Ryan says it adds to the sense the economy is losing momentum, and that's before the full impact of the emerging trade war with the U.S. starts to show up in hard data and likely pushes Canada into a mild recession mid-year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1341 GMT - Oil futures are lower but on track for a third straight weekly gain, with WTI hovering below the key $70 level. "Crude futures are still struggling to turn bullish," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. The coming seasonal ramp up in refineries to meet travel demand, and U.S. sanctionson Venezuelan and Iranian crude are supporting factors, but "the U.S. stock market is struggling, and longer-term demand fears are in the minds of most traders as tariffs begin to kick in on cars not manufactured in the U.S.," he says. WTI is off 0.2% at $69.77 a barrel, and Brent is 0.2% lower at $73.87 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1311 GMT - Canada's economy looks to have flatlined last month after stronger-than-expected growth to start the year, a possible early sign tariff worries are weighing. Statistics Canada forecasts industry-level GDP was little changed in February following growth of 0.4% on-month in January, the strongest expansion since last April. Economists were expecting growth of 0.3% for January, in line with the data agency's earlier flash estimate. January's growth was buoyed largely by both goods-producing industries, with a further rise in mining and oil and gas extraction and a rebound in manufacturing. The biggest drag on growth came from a pullback in retail trade, which the early data shows continued into February. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0921 GMT - Euro investment-grade credit spreads have tightened to nearly their tightest historical levels, boosting excess returns on the assets in the year to date, CreditSights analysts say in a note. On aggregate, the excess return gains on euro investment-grade bonds are plus 73 basis points year-to-date, comprised of 51 basis points from spread tightening and 22 basis points from carry, the analysts say. Some of the notable outperforming sectors are utilities, bank surbordinate bonds, insurance subordinate bonds, transportation and autos, CreditSights says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0832 GMT - SSE isn't expected to undergo a major strategic review under its incoming CEO Martin Pibworth, Jefferies analysts say in a note. Pibworth is well known to investors and has a deep expertise in energy markets and the execution of large capital projects, Jefferies says. "Overall, we see his appointment as a clear positive for SSE," the analysts say. Shares in the U.K. energy group are up 1.5% at 1,570 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1549 GMT - Rio Tinto investor and investment-management company Ninety One is confident that the miner's board has done the necessary work to assess the merits of collapsing its dual-listed structure and trusts its view that it poses risks to shareholders, Portfolio Manager George Cheveley told Dow Jones Newswires. "We acknowledge that the structure is not ideal but believe Rio Tinto is at an exciting strategic point and want to see the management team focused on operational delivery," he added. U.K. hedge fund Palliser has tabled resolutions calling for an independent review into the miner's structure at its annual general meetings. Ninety One Ltd. has a $120 million stake in Rio Tinto PLC--or under 0.2% of its shares--according to data from Refinitiv. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1433 GMT - A hotter-than expected February core PCE reading sparks new concerns about stagflation, and the role tariffs may play going forward. "We are moving in the wrong direction and the concern is that tariffs threaten higher prices, which mean the inflation prints are going to remain hot," says ING Economics in a note. "This will constrain the Fed's ability to deliver further interest rate cuts." These concerns have pushed commodity futures into the red across the board, with the exception of precious metals and copper--with gold surging above the $3,100 per troy ounce threshold, and copper climbing back towards a record-high close. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1420 GMT - The strength in Canada's economy at the start of the year won't alter the central bank's view that it will need to balance the deflationary impact of the tariffs due to weaker economic activity against inflationary pressures arising from higher costs with the tariffs, Alberta Central's Charles St-Arnaud says. And the weak preliminary February estimate, showing GDP was flat, can't be blamed entirely o U.S. tariff threats since the winter weather was unseasonably harsh, he adds. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1405 GMT - Economists are largely treating Canada's strong GDP increase in January with a shrug. The data, they say, is old news, given President Trump's tariffs to date on Canada aluminum and steel, and more to come next week, highlighted by a 25% levy on automobiles and auto parts. BMO Capital Markets chief economist Doug Porter says January's 0.4% month-over-month increase, and a flattening in February, puts 1Q GDP on the path toward 1.5%-to-2% annualized advance, or largely aligned with Bank of Canada expectations. "The rollicking start to the year is but cold comfort with tariffs now in place and poised to ramp up." Porter says the data show Canada's economy was solid ahead of the escalation in the trade conflict, and should reinforce BOC's "newfound lean to the sidelines" in April. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1311 GMT - Canada's economy looks to have flatlined last month after stronger-than-expected growth to start the year, a possible early sign tariff worries are weighing. Statistics Canada forecasts industry-level GDP was little changed in February following growth of 0.4% on-month in January, the strongest expansion since last April. Economists were expecting growth of 0.3% for January, in line with the data agency's earlier flash estimate. January's growth was buoyed largely by both goods-producing industries, with a further rise in mining and oil and gas extraction and a rebound in manufacturing. The biggest drag on growth came from a pullback in retail trade, which the early data shows continued into February. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1307 GMT - Canadian aluminum and steel producers, in the spotlight after the Trump administration slapped 25% tariffs on imports of the metals, rely heavily on trade between the countries. Exports to the U.S. accounted for 16.8% of Canadian GDP and more than 2.6 million Canadian jobs in 2023, the most recent year where value-added data is available. Statistics Canada notes alumina and aluminum production and processing industry had 68% of output attributed to direct exports to the U.S. Canada's steel industry and iron and steel mills and ferro-alloy manufacturing industry were also highly reliant on exports to the U.S., with 69% and 66% of their output attributed to U.S. demand, respectively. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1743 GMT - U.S. auto sales in March could top 1.45 million units, as consumers and automakers try to get ahead of tariffs, S&P Global Mobility projects. Chris Hopson, principal analyst at S&P Global Mobility, says "automakers, by way of incentives, and savvy consumers are likely attempting to get ahead of future uncertainty surrounding auto pricing levels by taking advantage of March deals." Hopson adds there are numerous downside risks to the auto demand and production environment given the potential for higher auto prices as a result of expected tariffs. The S&P Global Mobility US auto outlook for 2025 sees sustained, but more moderate growth levels for light vehicle sales, although consumer pressures and potential auto tariffs create notable downside risks to volume estimates at this time, the group says. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1601 GMT - Credit investor focus will be on U.S. tariffs announcements due on Wednesday, Societe Generale's Juan Valencia says in a note. Markets are more cautious after President Trump announced 25% tariffs on all auto imports into the U.S. An aggressive stance involving blanket tariffs across countries is likely to drive credit spreads volatility, Valencia says. "An announcement in line with market expectations or a little better should see credit spreads improve over the coming weeks," he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1411 GMT - The Canadian economy's strong start to 2025 should keep the central bank on hold in April, strategists at TD Securities argue. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1336 GMT - The winner from President Trump's plans to impose 25% tariffs on imported vehicles? No one, say analysts at Wedbush in a research note. While Trump is pushing for more auto manufacturing in the US, the analysts note that even US automakers that produce domestically have around half of their auto parts come from foreign markets. Tesla, for example, will be affected, as CEO Elon Musk himself acknowledged on his social media platform. "A US car with all US parts made in the US is a fictional tale not even possible today," say the analysts. They believe they will add up to $100 billion of costs annually for the auto industry, something that will be passed to the consumer and erode demand. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1331 GMT - Germany's labor market cooled further in March, and the situation could get worse, Deutsche Bank Research economist Marc Schattenberg says in a note. Seasonally adjusted unemployment ticked up to 6.3% from 6.2% in February, while the number of unemployed rose surprisingly sharply by around 26,000 to 2.92 million, he says. Job losses could persist, especially in the manufacturing sector, not least from the repercussions of U.S. trade policy on key German industries, Schattenberg says. Early labor market indicators don't point to any improvement in the coming months, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1329 GMT - Germany's labor market continued its gradual weakening in March as recruitment plans continue to shrink, ING's Carsten Brzeski says in a note. Seasonally adjusted unemployment rose by 26,000, increasing the unemployment rate to 6.3% from 6.2% in February. The unadjusted unemployment number of 2.967 million--albeit a fall from February--is the lowest March figure since the financial crisis, he says. Continued announcements of potential cost-cutting measures in the automotive and other industries are set to push up unemployment further, he says. The latest GfK consumer-confidence reading also published Friday suggests German consumers are focusing more on the cooling labor market than on politics even after elections brought added certainty, he says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1327 GMT - Michelin volumes remain subdued, but EPS is supported by pricing, cost discipline, buybacks, and low financial costs, HSBC analysts write. Following Michelin's February tire market volume data, the bank reduces its 2025 volume estimate by 1%. It expects the first quarter to be the weakest quarter due to tough comparables for light-vehicle tires and still-low demand for truck, construction, and mining tires. Nonetheless, HSBC models a progressive improvement throughout the year. "In our view, continued strength in free cash flow should support higher shareholder returns and be a positive catalyst for the stock." HSBC upgrades the stock to buy from hold and increases its target price to 38 euros from 36 euros. Shares rise 0.6% to 33.04 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1253 GMT - Ferrari has presented a clear and swift response to the 25% U.S. auto tariff hike, shouldering some of the burden while also making some price increases, Jefferies analyst James Grzinic writes. The bank estimates Ferrari's direct profit hit this year to be rather immaterial, at around 35 million euros, though the extent to which the balance of the pain will be divided up between dealers and clients is still unclear. "Perhaps the main take-away here is the group's unique ability to provide a very explicit response to such a major, and swift, impact on cost of goods sold." The fact that full-year earnings guidance was reiterated confirms the extent to which contingencies had been built into the outlook, Jefferies adds. Shares rise 3.8%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1041 GMT - Auto component manufacturer ZF is hoping for a quick agreement on U.S tariffs to avoid a new wave of protectionism that would affect the German company and have a negative global impact on growth, prosperity and jobs. ZF is investing $500 million in the expansion of its production plant in South Carolina as part of plans to bring more manufacturing closer to its customers and respond faster to changes in demand. "Our approach of producing as many products as possible locally in the respective markets proves to be correct and necessary," a ZF spokesperson said. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: US warns French companies they must comply with Trump's diversity ban
data.description:
The Trump administration has written to French companies holding U.S. government contracts ordering them to comply with an executive order banning diversity, equity and inclusion programmes, two people familiar with the matter said.

The companies have been told to confirm their compliance in a separate questionnaire entitled "Certification Regarding Compliance With Applicable Federal Anti-Discrimination Law." Reuters has seen a copy of the questionnaire.

The documents will raise concerns in European boardrooms that the Trump administration is widening its fight against DEI overseas, at a time U.S. President Donald Trump's actions on tariffs and security ties have upended trans-Atlantic relations.

French business daily Les Echos, which first reported the existence of the letter late on Friday, said the missive had been sent out to firms by the U.S. embassy in Paris.

An embassy spokesman did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

An official close to French Finance Minister Eric Lombard said the matter would be taken up with the U.S. government.

"This practice reflects the values of the new U.S. government. They are not the same as ours. The minister will remind his counterparts in the U.S. government of this," the official said.

Reuters could not immediately identify which companies had received the letter. Defence and infrastructure companies were among those that risked exposure, media reported.

It was not immediately clear if similar letters and questionnaires had been sent to foreign companies in other European countries.

Trump has sought to eradicate DEI initiatives, which he and other critics say are discriminatory, from the government and private sector.

data.description: AsiaHDPE: This week, High density polyethylene (HDPE) film and blow moulding grade prices gained in Far East Asia while they quoted stable in other parts of the Asian region. International crude oil prices are likely to experience a modest rise. Implementation of stricter U.S sanctions on Iranian crude oil exports, continuing production cuts by the OPEC+ alliance, and increasing geopolitical tensions could be cited as key factors supporting this rise. Recent U.S. sanctions and geopolitical uncertainties have amplified supply concerns, bolstering a favourable short-term outlook. HDPE prices firmed across specific grades owing to stronger import offers from overseas suppliers. In China, the polyethylene market continues to experience volatility, driven by cautious demand from downstream sectors and constrained purchasing power. Although supply pressures have diminished due to heightened maintenance activities, the overall supply-demand equilibrium is not expected to improve significantly in the near future. Market prices are experiencing slight fluctuations, with US dollar prices remaining stable. While US offer prices are considered reasonable, concerns regarding tariffs continue to linger. The increase in shipments from Iran is contributing to a rise in supply levels, and expectations for US dollar prices are on a downward trend, indicating that price fluctuations may remain weak in the short term. There is a potential rise in inventories due to incoming cargo from the US and new production capabilities coming up in China. A trader has noted that this situation may lead to buyers becoming less willing to acquire high-end cargo. Furthermore, although there has been a slight strengthening in downstream demand in China, the anticipated polyethylene supply from Exxon Huizhou and Inner Mongolia Baofeng could counterbalance this demand. In Southeast Asia, Import markets experienced a decline in demand as the majority of converters had already acquired adequate inventory and were not keen on restocking inventory. Suppliers from the Middle East made higher import offer announcements for April shipments, attributing this to a decrease in supply allocation; however, these offers attracted minimal interest from buyers. In Vietnam, a few transactions were completed within the expected price ranges, although reports of HDPE cargo offers from the United States continued to weigh on the overall sentiment. In Indonesia and Malaysia, demand further diminished as the market neared the final week of Ramadan and the forthcoming Eid ul-Fitr celebrations. Market participants are anticipating additional offers for April shipments that are expected in the coming week. A dearth of healthy factory orders and reluctance in acquiring raw materials suggest a cautious attitude among market participants. Additionally, middlemen were seen reducing prices to sell off inventory, highlighting the ongoing weak demand sentiments. In the absence of a definitive positive trigger, the current bearish outlook is likely to continue unless external influences alter the market conditions. In India, a lack of significant restocking interest has led to demand remaining steady, with some domestic purchases occurring to meet contract obligations ahead of the new financial year. Producers have offered discounts to expedite destocking activity and clear off accumulated inventories. In India, spot market activity has been quiet due to a scarcity of offers and sluggish demand, with only modest interest in imported goods seen following bookings early in the month. Market forecasts indicate that the shutdown of polyethylene production capacities by Indian producers could lead to supply shortages, which may subsequently heighten the demand for import cargoes. In Pakistan, the market for High density polyethylene (HDPE) film has experienced a downturn due to two primary factors: a surge in imports from Iran and a regulatory prohibition on single-use plastics in the Punjab province. The increase in Iranian imports has likely resulted in an oversupply, causing prices to drop and diminishing the demand for locally produced HDPE film, with consumers exhibiting a preference for import cargoes. Moreover, the ban on single-use plastics in Punjab has further constrained the HDPE film market, which has been widely utilized for various packaging purposes. As both consumers and businesses adjust to these new regulations, it is anticipated that the demand for such plastics will continue to decline. In Pakistan and Bangladesh, the demand for HDPE film continued to decline, as the last week of Ramadan drew near. The softened momentum can be linked to the seasonal decrease in purchasing activity, as both consumers and businesses typically scale back their spending in anticipation of the Eid ul-Fitr festivities. Consequently, the interplay of imports, regulatory modifications, and seasonal factors has led to a significant drop in the demand for HDPE film in the region. Implementation of stricter U.S sanctions on Iranian crude oil exports, continuing production cuts by the OPEC+ alliance, and increasing geopolitical tensions could be cited as key factors supporting this rise. However, importers have approached the procurement of cargoes set for April loading with caution, driven by a declining trend in the yuan-denominated market and concerns over unfavourable import margins. Market participants expect trading conditions to become more difficult in the near term. In Southeast Asia, the manufacturing sector is encountering increasing difficulties. Concurrently, companies from the US and Vietnam have finalized $4 billion in agreements related to energy and minerals. Malaysia is striving to expedite its shift towards chip design in light of the ongoing technological tensions between the US and China. Converters have indicated that brand owners are reducing their orders due to declining consumer spending, and the ongoing trade conflict with the US is leading to a decrease in orders destined for the US as buyers evaluate the effects of tariffs. In both Indonesia and Malaysia, demand has further diminished as the market nears the last week of Ramadan and the upcoming Eid ul-Fitr festivities. Market participants are expecting additional shipment offers for April in the coming week. In India, LDPE prices in the domestic market are rising despite a decrease in demand, as the global supply-demand dynamics continue to support price increases. Manufacturers are concentrating on need-based production, resulting in slight price variations due to reduced sales volumes. Although inventory levels are sufficient, the demand for LDPE in India remains weak. The decline in oil prices raises concerns about future price trends, although forthcoming demand patterns may offer clearer insights. Currently, no major risks are foreseen, aside from a possible significant decline in oil prices. Traders anticipate that actual demand will improve in April. In Pakistan, the availability of LDPE film remained limited, while demand decreased due to imports from Iran and a prohibition on single-use plastics in the Punjab province. Buyers closely tracked the Indian market, expecting an uptick in import demand in April. In Sri Lanka, prices marginally increased due to limited allocations from Middle Eastern suppliers. However, the purchase pulse remained muted, with buyers staying on the sidelines due to weak end-product demand. Nevertheless, purchasing activity remained subdued, as buyers opted to stay on the sidelines in response to low demand for end products. The general demand sentiment has been stable, accompanied by moderate restocking activities. PP: This week, Polypropylene prices were left unchanged in the Asian region. A minor recovery was noted in conjunction with increasing crude oil futures. Meanwhile, Malaysia's PRefChem has been maintaining its PP output steady amid availability of spot offers. In Thailand, reports indicate that IRPC has temporarily halted operations at two of its PP units for maintenance in March. Nevertheless, the local supply remains stable, attributed to a weak downstream demand. In India, polypropylene prices experienced an increase, driven by enhanced buying sentiment and a reduction in supply resulting from capacity shutdowns in the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) region. Strong offers and restocking efforts undertaken in anticipation of higher downstream consumption are providing short-term support to the market. Demand has remained consistent, although restocking interest has been limited. Producers have been offering discounts as they continue to destock before the conclusion of the current financial year. Nevertheless, the sales ratio has seen a slight decline due to increasing competition among granule manufacturers. PP producers are continuing with regular purchase activities, focusing on sales rather than stockpiling, which indicates a cautious approach within the market. In Pakistan, market activity remained quiet during the week, affected by a dominant bearish sentiment. Offers originating from China emerged at lower price levels; nonetheless, the decline of the local currency against the US dollar weakened buying interest. In spite of political instability and currency depreciation, market sentiment in the country experienced a mid-week uptick. PS: Despite weaker feedstock SM prices, PS prices were assessed largely steady in the Asian region this week. International crude oil prices are likely to experience a modest rise. Implementation of stricter U.S sanctions on Iranian crude oil exports, continuing production cuts by the OPEC+ alliance, and increasing geopolitical tensions could be cited as key factors supporting this rise. Recent U.S. sanctions and geopolitical uncertainties have amplified supply concerns, bolstering a favourable short-term outlook. The market continued to be primarily influenced by costs. With the momentum in the feedstock styrene monomer (SM) market remaining sluggish, regional buyers sought more advantageous pricing for cargo to align with the fluctuations in costs. Leading polystyrene (PS) manufacturers in Asia have been focusing on export markets due to increased competition from Chinese products within the region, aiming to direct larger quantities to South Asia and deep-sea markets. This trend is expected to continue in the short term. In China, the PS import market continues to be relatively quiet due to a lack of definitive purchasing interest. Only those buyers with particular quality specifications or re-export needs are likely to consider importing cargoes. In the spot market, China is a prominent exporter of GPPS, particularly to Southeast Asia, owing to logistical advantages. The competitive cost structure of production, especially for integrated facilities with feedstock SM lines, has enhanced the economic appeal of PS cargoes from China. The assessment remains unchanged from the previous week, reflecting the absence of substantial discussions and the overall stability of quotes from regional suppliers. In Southeast Asia, discussions in the spot market persisted, although the momentum experienced a slight decline compared to the previous week, as reported by market participants. The relatively advantageous pricing of polystyrene (PS) in comparison to the second half of 2024, influenced by fluctuations in production costs, attracted some buyers to either proceed with or expedite their replenishment activities. Similar to the prior week, suppliers continued to make minor concessions in pricing to secure deals; however, their production margins largely remained unaffected, as the decrease in production costs was the primary reason for the price reductions. Regional suppliers, aware of the cost-sensitive purchasing behaviour, maintained flexibility in spot pricing, particularly for buyers with substantial demand. Additionally, some players in the PS market noted the competitive pricing and aggressive sales tactics of Chinese-origin products in Southeast Asia. While it proved challenging for certain regional PS suppliers to compete with the prices of imports from China, they focused on alternative markets such as South Asia and Europe to sustain sales amid increasing competition. Despite an improvement in market liquidity over the past weeks, driven by cost-related market adjustments, some regional market participants adopted a cautious stance regarding long-term market prospects. They expressed concerns that the recent liquidity boost was primarily due to buyers advancing their scheduled purchases to secure favourable pricing, rather than indicating a systematic enhancement in demand. With consumption in key sectors, such as packaging and appliances, showing minimal structural improvements, buyers' strategies may suggest an early depletion of future demand, complicating suppliers' planning for production and inventory management. In India, the approaching conclusion of the fiscal year in India on March 31 has continued to restrain import activities, as purchasers prioritize meeting domestic procurement obligations and reducing inventory levels. The supply of PVC in China is tightening in anticipation of a significant maintenance schedule set for April, although production of carbide-based PVC has remained consistent. Exports have been restricted due to price expectations from Taiwan's Formosa being lower than anticipated for the Indian market. Although a few producers have lowered their prices to bolster sales, yet overall trading activity has been lacklustre. Market observers have noted a possible rise in freight rates from North China to India for shipments in April, although current rates have remained stable. In Southeast Asia, import prices have remained consistent this week, attributed to reduced regional demand and sufficient inventories among converters. Outside of Vietnam, there was limited buying interest for Taiwanese offers, primarily due to the presence of more affordable alternatives. Similarly, South Korean offers to Malaysia experienced sluggish demand, as buyers opted for more competitively priced options available in the market. In Vietnam, prices for Chinese ethylene-based products remained stable, while Thai offers recorded confirmed transactions at slightly reduced rates. Market participants are bracing for possible increases in freight costs from China following Ramadan and the Thai New Year, although immediate forecasts remain unclear. Overall, the Southeast Asian market is experiencing muted demand, with many converters depleting their existing stock. These offers are lower by USD 40/mt from the offer made by the producer in the previous month. These offers are for April 2025 shipment on CIF Nhava Sheva/Mundra/Chennai ports basis (LC at Sight). A rigorous maintenance schedule throughout the Asia-Pacific region in the second quarter, along with possible increases in freight costs and heightened demand prior to the monsoon season, may contribute to rising import prices. Nevertheless, market dynamics will primarily hinge on the evolution of BIS regulations and the ongoing discussions regarding anti-dumping measures. Additionally, Government of India has imposed anti-dumping duty on Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) Paste Resin imports from China, Korea RP, Malaysia, Norway, Taiwan, and Thailand. Asian polyethylene terephthalate (PET) prices remained stable due to a lack of strong buying interest and an abundant supply in the spot market, which kept pressure on pricing. se buying interest.
