Concerns regarding US import tariffs intensified among converters, with some only able to obtain technical-grade LLDPE from the US. Throughout the week, traders were reluctant to make offers, with some completely suspending US imports until there was more clarity on the tariffs. Others indicated they would refrain from offering materials from shipments anticipated next month. European contract prices for polyethylene LLDPE have remained firm, with variations influenced by demand for specific grades and regional supply conditions. A slight rise in monomer prices has set the stage for pricing negotiations. The robust market activity observed in January and February resulted in increased initial offers at the beginning of March; however, as the month advanced, buying activity diminished considerably. Weaker demand from downstream sectors, especially packaging and industrial, contributed to a slowdown in transactions, while buyers exercised caution due to economic uncertainties. Although the potential announcement of US import tariffs did not have an immediate effect on market operations in March, its impact is anticipated to become more significant in April. Major buyers, particularly those dependent on US imports, have started to explore European alternatives. Nonetheless, these discussions remain largely preliminary, as many participants in the market are waiting for more definitive information regarding tariff policies before finalizing their sourcing strategies. In addition, feedstock dynamics have been crucial in maintaining price stability. Ethylene availability has remained consistent throughout the region, while naphtha prices have sent mixed signals. Supply chain challenges, including logistical issues and port congestion in certain areas, have also influenced purchasing behavior. Looking forward, the European LLDPE market is entering a pivotal phase, as demand patterns, tariff decisions, and fluctuations in feedstock prices will play a significant role in shaping price trends and trade flows in the upcoming months. PP: This week, PP spot prices fell while contract prices were assessed steady in the European region. The European polypropylene (PP) market ended the month on a subdued note as buyers began to look ahead, bracing for weak demand and the forthcoming monomer contract pricing for April. Demand from key sectors like construction and automotive derivatives remained low, hindered by persistent macroeconomic difficulties and reduced public expenditure. While a seasonal uptick in construction demand was anticipated, the overall market sentiment continued to be weak. Domestic supply was mostly stable, with only a few ongoing outages affecting availability. Nevertheless, the combination of sufficient supply and lacklustre demand resulted in a slight decrease in spot prices for both homopolymer and copolymer grades. Market activity was notably quiet throughout the week, as many participants opted to postpone spot purchases until April, driven by widespread expectations of a drop in contract pricing for essential feedstock propylene. Market observers have predicted a reduction in the April propylene contract price, following a downturn in upstream naphtha prices. PS: This week, PS contract prices climbed, while spot prices remained flat in the European region. European polystyrene (PS) contract prices saw an increase as the month's settlements were concluded. However, as the month advanced, some negotiations faced delays attributed to falling styrene spot prices, with reports suggesting that final settlements ended up lower than the styrene contract delta. Overall, demand for polystyrene remained low, although the packaging sector demonstrated some resilience during the month. Looking forward, a converter indicated that demand for ice cream packaging is anticipated to grow with the approach of summer. The construction sector continued to face challenges, particularly with weak activity reported in Northwest Europe. Furthermore, purchasing trends among end-users significantly influenced polystyrene consumption. Economic uncertainty has negatively impacted demand for white appliances, which has, in turn, affected the expandable polystyrene (EPS) market, as EPS is frequently utilized in the packaging and transportation of these appliances. Imports into Europe persisted, although a converter mentioned that many of these shipments were ordered two months earlier and had only recently arrived due to shipping delays. Market participants generally foresee stable demand in the upcoming months. However, a converter in Southern Europe pointed out that April may be a shorter trading month due to several holidays in Italy, leading them to prepare for smaller-volume purchases. ABS: This week, ABS prices marched higher in the European region. The European acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS) market has shown overall improvement, driven by consistent demand from the household appliance sector and encouraging signs of recovery in related industries. Although the automotive sector encountered some difficulties, the market demonstrated resilience, characterized by stable order volumes and growing interest from the construction and furniture sectors. Anticipated seasonal demand fluctuations are likely to bolster a stronger performance in the second quarter, as market stakeholders foresee enhancements with the uptick in economic activity. Supply conditions remain robust, with no reported production halts or disruptions. Domestic demand has been steadily rising, and European suppliers are experiencing heightened interest as buyers increasingly favor reliable and regional sourcing. While import activity has slightly declined, it continues to influence market dynamics. The rise in import prices, prompted by the European Commission's anti-dumping investigation into materials from South Korea and UNV1, has led European producers to adopt more competitive pricing strategies. Consequently, domestic purchasing activity has intensified, contributing to a more balanced and stable market environment. Despite the impact of seasonal holidays in April on short-term trading, the overall sentiment for the second quarter remains positive. Market participants anticipate steady demand growth, bolstered by enhanced confidence in supply chain stability and pricing dynamics, which support an optimistic outlook. PVC: This week, PVC contract prices firmed while spot prices quoted flat in the European region. The European Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) contract market experienced an increase due to ongoing high electricity prices and producers reporting unsustainable profit margins. In the contract market, producers successfully negotiated price hikes with consumers for March. However, the initially proposed larger increases at the beginning of the month were reduced through negotiations with consumers, who cited weak downstream demand and competitively priced imports from Asia. The persistent high costs of electricity continued to be a major concern for producers, putting additional pressure on their margins. Moreover, the volatility in energy prices complicated forward planning. While the elevated electricity costs were a significant issue, the unpredictable pricing environment presented an even greater challenge for producers. In the spot market, some participants opted for a cautious approach, waiting for the resolution of the European ethylene industry's monthly contract price (CP) for April, which is anticipated to be announced late this week or early next week. In March, demand remained stable, mirroring the levels observed in January and February, as seasonal purchasing interest from the construction sector had not yet emerged. Spain experienced a higher demand relative to other European markets, attributed to a rise in construction activities. Looking forward, some analysts forecasted a minor decline in European PVC demand in April, influenced by the Easter holidays. Interest in imports from Asia continued, with traders securing agreements for arrivals in June. Furthermore, some clients were performing quality assessments on Asian PVC. Nevertheless, certain market participants expressed concerns regarding quality and logistical challenges, which diminished the attractiveness of Asian imports. Throughout March, imports from Mexico to Europe decreased due to disruptions in the supply of vinyl chloride monomer from the US to Mexico earlier in the year. This limited availability is expected to persist into April, with a return to normal supply levels anticipated in May. PET: This week, PET contract prices increased, while spot prices remained flat in the European region. European PET contract prices strengthened in March, driven by a consistently tight feedstock market and a shift in trading focus towards contract agreements instead of the spot market. The increased dependence on imports, coupled with interruptions in domestic production, exerted upward pressure on prices. As a result, March contract prices increased compared to February, as producers aimed to transfer higher feedstock costs to customers, particularly for Purified terephthalic acid (PTA) and Monoethylene glycol (MEG), both of which remained in limited supply. The constraints on domestic production, due to both planned and unplanned outages, compelled buyers to secure materials through long-term contracts, thereby diminishing liquidity in the spot market. Additionally, the tightness in feedstock, along with disruptions in freight and logistics�such as extended lead times for imported materials�further contributed to the rise in prices. Asian imports, which usually alleviate supply pressures in Europe, faced fluctuating freight rates and heightened competition from demand in other regions. Market participants observed steady demand in the beverage and packaging industries, with some seasonal restocking in anticipation of peak summer consumption. However, overarching macroeconomic concerns and cautious purchasing behavior among downstream buyers moderated aggressive buying activity. Looking forward, the outlook for PET pricing in April will hinge on trends in feedstock prices, the stability of supply chains, and any potential recovery in regional production capacity. Market participants will closely watch producers' strategies for passing on costs and the effects of any new developments on the supply side. In spite of this currency instability, a substantial influx of Asian PET is projected to reach Europe in April, coinciding with the onset of the preform manufacturing season. USHDPE: This week, HDPE Injection export prices declined sharply in the US. US spot export prices for high-density polyethylene (HDPE) have decreased across all grades over the past week, driven by a pessimistic market outlook and ongoing uncertainty that has subdued buying interest. Market participants have observed that the current ambiguity regarding possible tariff changes has negatively impacted export sentiment, resulting in a backwardated market structure. Traders and buyers are exercising caution, expecting further price reductions due to a projected rise in supply. Concerns about a potential oversupply have further dampened the market outlook, discouraging purchasing activity. Additionally, declining feedstock prices have added to the overall downward pressure, reinforcing the expectation that HDPE prices may continue to weaken in the near future. As market conditions remain dynamic, participants are closely watching price movements and potential changes in trade policy that could affect demand recovery. Until more clarity is provided, the prevailing sentiment is likely to remain cautious, with buyers reluctant to make forward commitments. LDPE: This week, LDPE export prices decreased in the US. US spot export prices for low-density polyethylene (LDPE) have experienced a decline over the past week, influenced by weak market fundamentals and persistent uncertainty. Disruptions in the supply chain have resulted in considerable backlogs within the US export market, leading to delivery delays and contributing to the overall sluggishness of the market. Buyer sentiment remains cautious, with many potential purchasers hesitant to make commitments due to concerns regarding possible tariff changes. Market participants report that this uncertainty has fostered a wait-and-see attitude, further suppressing trading activity. As March comes to an end, producers have started to present their offers for April. However, sources indicate that these offer levels have largely remained stable, reflecting a lack of demand. Market sources mentioned that if producers wish to sustain current price levels, they will need to adjust production output to better match the ongoing weakness in the export market. Without such adjustments, pricing pressures may persist in the upcoming weeks. Market participants anticipate that an increase in domestic capacity will gradually enhance demand, potentially leading to sustained price increases in the months ahead. LLDPE: This week, weaker upstream ethylene rates pulled LLDPE export prices sharply lower in the US. US spot export prices for low linear density polyethylene (LLDPE) have declined across all grades over the past week, primarily due to weak buying interest and a surplus of supply linked to ongoing uncertainties surrounding tariffs. Market participants have indicated that demand is still low, as buyers are reluctant to make commitments in the face of an ambiguous trade landscape. Previously, supply constraints had limited availability through late February and into March, but these issues are beginning to subside. While some sources still indicate tight availability, others observe that certain volumes are re-entering the market. As supply gradually stabilizes and demand continues to lack vigor, market sentiment remains cautious. Participants are closely watching forthcoming producer offers and potential changes in trade policy that could further impact pricing trends in the coming weeks. PP: This week, PP export prices rolled over in the US. US spot export prices for polypropylene (PP) have remained unchanged both from the previous day and the prior week, as international buyers continue to seek alternative sourcing options due to ongoing uncertainties surrounding tariffs. Participants in the market have observed that changing trade dynamics and cautious purchasing approaches have resulted in limited transactions, with many clients reluctant to commit to resin sourced from the US. Polypropylene from Asia continues to be the most competitively priced in the global market, although this is subject to variations in freight rates. Buyers generally consider US materials only when shipping costs from Asia become excessively high. As freight rates play a crucial role in influencing buying decisions, market sentiment remains variable, with the demand for US PP closely tied to global logistics and pricing trends. PS: This week, PS export prices down adjusted in the US. US polystyrene (PS) prices experienced a decline over the past week, influenced by a significant decrease in upstream benzene prices, which have reached their lowest point in over four years. This reduction in feedstock costs has exerted additional downward pressure on the PS market, compounded by weak demand fundamentals. Domestic consumption of PS has remained sluggish, with no indications of an increase in purchasing activity. Market observers have noted that end-user demand has not demonstrated any substantial recovery, resulting in a subdued market sentiment. Given the price reductions driven by costs and the lack of robust demand, the US PS market is currently in a cautious wait-and-see phase as participants evaluate short-term pricing trends. PVC: This week, PVC export prices dropped marginally in the US. The US polyvinyl chloride (PVC) market experienced a price decline over the past week, primarily due to ongoing weak demand, especially from Latin America. Market participants indicate that purchasing interest is low, with no immediate signs of recovery. Consequently, industry experts largely anticipate that US PVC export prices will persist in their downward trajectory in the upcoming weeks. Traders attribute this pessimistic outlook to several factors, including sluggish global demand and strategic pricing manoeuvres by US manufacturers. The pricing strategy implemented by one US producer in April has set a precedent, leading to expectations that further price reductions may be necessary in May, as there are few viable options to boost demand. Compounding the uncertainty, discussions regarding potential tariff changes have surfaced as a significant factor affecting market sentiment. Some analysts believe that alterations in trade policies could influence export competitiveness, presenting additional hurdles for US suppliers. Moreover, competition from PVC sourced from Asia remains a significant concern, particularly along the West Coast of South America. Buyers in this region are actively seeking more affordable alternatives, with Asian suppliers providing competitively priced shipments. This intensifying competitive pressure is further limiting US export prospects, resulting in an increasingly uncertain market outlook. PET: This week, PET prices were left unchanged in the US. The US polyethylene terephthalate (PET) spot market has exhibited stability over the past week, with minimal changes in market fundamentals. Buying activity has been lacklustre, primarily due to ongoing weak demand from end consumers, which has negatively impacted market sentiment. Uncertainties in trade, along with broader economic issues such as persistent inflation and elevated interest rates, have suppressed demand for PET products, especially in the packaging and bottled water sectors. Market observers have pointed out that PET consumption is heavily influenced by consumer spending trends, which have not yet demonstrated significant improvement. As economic challenges continue to restrict demand growth, the PET market remains in a cautious state, with stakeholders closely observing macroeconomic developments and potential changes in consumer purchasing habits.

data.title: China to provide Myanmar with $13.8 million in quake aid, embassy says
data.description:
China will provide Myanmar with 100 million yuan ($13.77 million) worth of aid after an earthquake that killed more than 1,000 people, its embassy said on Saturday.

The aid will include tents, blankets, emergency medical kits, food and water and other essential items, with the first batch arriving March 31, according to the Chinese embassy's Facebook page.

data.title: Taking RMDs Can Be Tricky. Here's the Right Way.
data.description:
By Karen Hube

Key decisions loom for people aged 73 and over who must take required minimum distributions (RMDs) from retirement accounts.

Extreme stock market volatility lately raises questions about when to take RMDs. Is it better to spread them out during the tax year or wait until year-end?

Meanwhile, if you turned 73 last year and haven't yet taken your 2024 RMD, you have no option to wait: April 1 is the last day to draw your first RMD and have it count for 2024 — If you don't, penalties will begin accruing.

Annual minimum distributions are required from tax-deferred accounts such as individual retirement accounts (IRA)s, 401(k)s and 403(b)s once you turn 73.

For the year you turn 73, your RMD can be postponed until April 1 of the following year, but every year after it must be taken within the calendar year. If you postponed last year's RMD to April 1, you will have to take both your 2024 and 2025 RMDs by the end of this year.

There's an exception for workplace retirement plans: If you are still working and own no more than 5% of the business sponsoring your plan, you can hold off taking RMDs from that plan until you retire — no matter what age you are.

The amount of your RMD is determined by a life expectancy factor established by the Internal Revenue Service, based on the age you will be at year-end, and how long you are estimated to live.

You calculate your RMD by dividing your account value on Dec. 31 of the prior year by your life expectancy factor.

If your IRA assets were valued at $300,000 on Dec. 31 last year and you will be 75 at year-end, your life expectancy factor is 24.6, and your RMD for 2025 is $12,195.

You can look up your life expectancy factor on the IRS's website and calculate your RMD yourself or use an RMD calculator, such as one on the U.S. Securities and Exchange website ( https://www.investor.gov/financial-tools-calculators/calculators/required-minimum-distribution-calculator).

Your life expectancy factor will change every year, along with the size of your RMD.

If you have multiple retirement accounts, you must calculate the RMD for each one.

If you have multiple IRAs, you can tally up your RMDs and take the total from one account. The same goes for 403(b)s.

But if you have multiple 401(k)s and 457(b)s, you must take your RMDs separately from each account.

To wait or not to wait?

People who rely RMDs to live on typically take them either early in the year or in monthly or quarterly installments; others often wait until year-end so their money can grow tax-deferred for as long as possible.

If the stock market takes a nosedive when you're planning an early-year or regular withdrawal, "you may want to tap the brakes if you have other resources to pay bills," says Mark Parthemer, Glenmede's chief wealth strategist.

Taking your RMD in a down market means you have to sell more shares of stocks to fulfill the requirement.

While you may want to delay an RMD during a substantial market downswing, it's best not to habitually time your RMDs, especially if you live on them in retirement, says Jane Ditelberg, director of tax planning at Northern Trust.

Taking a lump sum either early or late in the year also has potential downsides. Early withdrawals miss out on tax-deferred growth if the market rises. And if you wait until December and the market slides, you can't delay your distribution. Waiting to take your entire RMD until year-end is also risky. If the market slides in December, you can't delay your distribution.

The safest bet for most investors is to spread RMDs throughout the year either quarterly or monthly for the best potential average results, Ditelberg says. "You'll get some high days and some low days in the market."

Consider what assets to take out

If you must take your RMD in a down market, one option is to leave battered stocks in your account to allow them time to recover. Instead pull out cash or assets that have held up in value.

Another option is to transfer depressed stockholdings from a tax-deferred account into a taxable account without liquidating them, says Ashley Weeks, a wealth strategist at TD Wealth. That still satisfies RMD rules. And although you will pay income taxes on the value of the transferred stock, by keeping the positions invested you can benefit from a future market rebound.

"Then your cost basis is re-established in a taxable account and future growth will be taxed as long-term capital gains (at a top rate of 20%)," Weeks says. By contrast, withdrawals from tax-deferred accounts are taxed as ordinary income with tax rates up to 37%.

Beware other risks

There are risks to year-end RMDs that have nothing to do with market fluctuations, Weeks says.

"Things could happen that could prevent you from taking your RMD. The holidays. You could forget or get sick," Weeks says.

The penalty for not taking an RMD is 25%. If you correct your mistake and file an amended tax return within two years, the penalty is reduced to 10%.

Turn your RMD into a charitable contribution

If you don't need your RMD to live on, consider making a contribution from your IRA once you are over age 70 1/2 .

Every year you can make what's called a qualified charitable distribution from your IRA account — this year the maximum is $108,000. It counts as part or all of your RMD as long as it goes directly to the nonprofit.

You don't get to claim a charitable deduction on your tax return, but you don't have to pay income taxes on the distribution.

If the charity wants to receive stock, you can maximize the number of shares you donate by doing so when the stock market is in a downswing, Ditelberg says.

But if a charity wants to hold cash, to keep more shares growing in your account "you want to give when the value of your account is high," she says.

Write to editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

REP. MIKE FLOOD (R-NE) IS INTERVIEWED ON CNN`S THE LEAD

MARCH 28, 2025

SPEAKERS:

REP. MIKE FLOOD (R-NE)

PHIL MATTINGLY, CNN HOST

PHIL MATTINGLY, CNN HOST: I want to turn now to Republican Congressman Mike Flood of Nebraska.

Congressman, really appreciate your time. You notably held a town hall in person over the last recess. You're talking to constituents, you're meeting with constituents face to face. You're shaking their hands. We saw the videos from it. Are you hearing concern right now about the direction of the economy?

REP. MIKE FLOOD (R-NE): Well, to be honest, I think Nebraskans, we know that tariffs are going to cause an adjustment period to take place. But we saw this during the first Trump administration where President Trump successfully used tariffs to produce great trade deals for the U.S. President Biden didn't do anything in trade for four years. And so now we're back at the table and we're hoping for better deals. We're looking for better deals with Mexico, with China and other countries.

Certainly, our partners up north in Canada, we've seen this before. And I think at the end of the day, this is going to work out.

MATTINGLY: To your point, Congressman, in farmers, the AG community, I think writ large, if you look at polling, supports President Trump. There's no question about that. However, in that first term, that China phase one trade deal never came anywhere close to what was actually agreed on in terms of China's purchases of agricultural products. And also, farmers had to be bailed out to the tune of tens of billions of dollars from the federal government because of those tariffs. Why is this time different?

FLOOD: Well, Joe Biden's in action for four years. Let's just talk about white corn in Mexico. The Mexican president's wife at the time basically claimed that there was some genetic issue with our white corn, which is not based in any kind of science. They just arbitrarily shut it off. And we couldn't get President Biden to stand in front of a podium or anything, microphone, to do anything about it.

Well, now we have a president that's engaged. We have a president that's getting the attention of these neighbors that have in some cases taken advantage of it. And ultimately, it's my hope that this results in expanded trade, breaks down barriers so that we can export more of our great products around the world.

Listen, if we don't have world markets for our corn and our beans, our beef, we don't have much to sell outside of that. Nebraska, we're going to be hurting. So we want that — we want these trade deals to get done. And we do believe Donald Trump can do it. We've seen it before, he'll do it again.

MATTINGLY: And it's been fascinating to watch. Ahead of the reciprocal tariffs, you see the E.U., other countries trying to come in beforehand say, all right, fine, we'll negotiate. It's having an effect on some level. I think the question to the point you're making is we know the outcome. We know the Trump administration's theory of the case. The process to get from right now to their adjustment period is the term you use.

How long is it going to take?

FLOOD: Well, let's just take a look at — you talked about the E.U. You know, American auto manufacturers, we face a 10 percent tariff from the European Union. Well, we only have a 2.5 percent tariff on their cars. At the end of the day, it's going to take as long as it is to get it right. And you don't get people's attention unless you start calling them out.

I think the first time that President Trump did this, it lasted just long enough to make things good and to make things work. And we saw the economy boom. If we can get these tariffs fixed, if we can build relationships where we're selling American products, oh, and by the way, at the end of the day, we need to be a country that is making things. This isn't just about other countries. Hopefully this is incentivizing more production, more manufacturing to be a country that produces things and not relying on countries like China that don't want us to be the world's sole superpower anymore.

Like, there's a lot on the line here.

MATTINGLY: Yes.

FLOOD: It's bigger than just trade. It's bigger than the economy. This is also a national security issue.

MATTINGLY: I could certainly talk econ with you all day, but I do want to ask a political question, just because on Tuesday we're going to actually see some political races that everybody's paying a lot of attention to. Three critical races across the U.S., Wisconsin Supreme Court race, two congressional races in Florida to fill Matt Gaetz's seat, Mike Waltz's seat. These races come after Republicans have lost local races. A series of local races so far this year, two races in Pennsylvania, one in Iowa. Are you concerned right now?

FLOOD: Well, I came in a special election, and there's nothing special about it. These are unpredictable situations. So my race was the Tuesday. My election was the Tuesday after the Dobbs decision was handed down from the Supreme Court. It was a very volatile time politically and obviously depends on what the news of the day is.

But the biggest problem with these special elections is that a great majority of people that are very good citizens, they're responsible people, they just don't know there's an election. And so a lot of times it hinges on who's the most motivated to get out. Now, there's a lot of attention on the race down in Florida, but at the end of the day, it's anybody's guess as how some of these will go. I'm confident from what I hear from my Florida colleagues in the United States Congress that we're going to be celebrating a victory. If somebody says, oh, but it wasn't as big of a victory, I don't buy that.

I won by six points in a special and I won by 16 points in a general. Special elections are just different.

MATTINGLY: There is no question about that.

Republican Congressman Mike Flood, really appreciate your time, sir. Thank you.

FLOOD: Have a good day.

END

MARCH 28, 2025

SPEAKERS:

REP. PAT RYAN (D-NY)

KASIE HUNT, CNN HOST

KASIE HUNT, CNN HOST: Joining us now, Democratic Congressman Pat Ryan of New York. He's a member of the Senate Armed — excuse me, the House Armed Services Committee and, of course, a veteran of the U.S. army.

Sir, thanks very much for being here.

REP. PAT RYAN (D-NY): Thanks for having me.

HUNT: Let me start there with what Pam Bondi said. She seems to think that Signal cannot be hacked. Your response?

RYAN: It's delusional. It's devoid of all reality. I was an Army intelligence officer 27 months in combat. That's just factually incorrect. And I would hope she'd go get a briefing from a host of national security — different national security agencies, including the NSA, that would tell her differently than that.

HUNT: If one of your commanders or somebody above you was communicating this way about the things that you were doing in the field, how would you have felt about that?

RYAN: Betrayed. I think this is another betrayal, honestly, by this administration, both in what it did, but then in just flat out — rather than taking accountability, they're flat out not even covering it up, but just lying directly to the American people saying Signal secure, saying there weren't sources and methods, saying there wasn't classified information, all verifiably, demonstrably — demonstrably false, things for an administration that claimed to run on confidence and strength.

And that has now been eviscerated this week in a way that I think is going to have lasting and should have lasting political and really moral effects with our allies. If I had disclosed that kind of sensitive operational information, regardless of the means, I would lose my clearance, I would be punished under the Uniform Code of Military Justice, and I would potentially go to jail.

And why should the secretary of defense or any other senior leader not be held to the same standard?

HUNT: One of the things that we reported in this story is about John Ratcliffe and his ability to continue to do the job, one source say that — says that, quote, I think he's going to be viewed skeptically for using the app for that purpose. And they're talking about kind of inside Langley, inside the building.

Do you think that's true, or would you grade the way Ratcliffe handled his existence in the chat differently from some of the other officials?

RYAN: I think any trained professional would should have immediately known that using what is clearly not a secure and commercial platform anywhere, in particularly one of them being in Moscow, puts the whole group at risk. And so, any professional should have flagged that immediately and proactively said, guys, we need to move this somewhere else.

The fact that none of them did that to me shows this is not an isolated incident, that we can only imagine the number of other similar small group or other chats, and that needs to be investigated.

I've called for investigations in the House Armed Services Committee, which they've turned down. Pam Bondi is saying she's refusing to look at this despite a longstanding bipartisan precedent to do otherwise. And so, this is now moving from screw up, I'd use another word, but were on TV, to cover up. And that's a real problem for this administration.

HUNT: It's cable, you know.

Sir, let me ask you about the Democratic Party here, because we started off the show looking at some signs that perhaps President Trump is reaping some of what he's sown within the first few days here. Theres some concern about these special elections in deep red areas that suggests that perhaps Democrats may have, you know, be sort of regrouping. There was a special election in Pennsylvania, a very, you know, small data point in Pennsylvania that was a good result for Democrats.

My question to you is if there is, in fact, this backlash, do you think it's because Democrats have done anything that voters should give Democrats credit for? Or do you not deserve that credit?

RYAN: Talking to voters in my district, which is a very swingy purple district, they're frankly fed up with both parties and have lost — continually, consistently lost trust in both parties. And what you're seeing in the reaction to this and conversations I've had and data I've had on the Signal thing is, it's just another example of elected officials being dishonest and not taking accountability when people in their normal lives, you got to take accountability for whether you're delivering for your family or delivering at work.

And so, the opportunity for Democrats is to lean into our — the trust that we have built and — and to be honest and authentic and say, yeah, we've made mistakes. When we do, we take accountability. We have investigations, we fire people that screw up.

This administration has a chance to do that. Trump is, of course, you know, built his — a lot of the facade of his reputation around using that phrase, you're fired. Maybe he should do that with one of the multiple people who screwed up on this chain.

HUNT: Very briefly. Do you think President Trump has done anything right in office so far?

RYAN: I had hopes on a few things that he would, you know, taxes on tips and social security, lowering costs. He's literally done the exact opposite. And we're seeing that in my district. People are as costs are going up, small business owners and families are getting punished. So, I wish I could say yes because I'm certainly rooting for the country, but I don't think he has.

HUNT: All right. Congressman Pat Ryan, thanks very much for coming in. I hope you'll come back soon. And happy birthday as well.

END

I think you mentioned the town hall you had earlier today. What are you hearing from those people? What do they want?

SCHOLTEN: Yes. You know, they want to see action. And this is why, again, I was not afraid to go out there and listen to people. And even Republicans are saying, Democrats, please do something right now. You know, we feel like this is not what we voted for. You know, Elon Musk is unaccountable. He has no transparency, no accountability. You know, he's slashing, you know, our essential services and programs and they want to see action from the Democratic Party.

I'm making sure to go out and reassure people that I'm fighting for them every single day, that I am committed to protecting Social security, Medicare, Medicaid, and that we're going to use every tool in the toolbox to stand up for them.

MATTINGLY: Democratic Congresswoman Hillary Scholten, I'm very proud of our ability as a Buckeye and a Michigander to have a cordial conversation.

More town halls like that would be great. I appreciate your time, ma'am. Thanks so much.

SCHOLTEN: Nothing's (inaudible).

MATTINGLY: Thanks.

END

data.title: Adani Green Energy' subsidiary starts 37.5 MW solar power project in Gujarat’s Khavda
data.description:
Adani Green Energy Ltd (AGEL) said on March 29 that its wholly-owned subsidiary, Adani Renewable Energy Fifty Seven Ltd, has commissioned a 37.5 MW solar power project at Khavda in Gujarat.

With the latest development, the company's total renewable energy capacity has risen to 13,737.8 MW, Adani Green Energy said in a regulatory filing on March 29.

Also read: SAMIL seeks to soothe investors as Trump tariff worries weigh on auto stocks

A day earlier, AGEL secured a significant 400 MW solar power project in Rajasthan through its subsidiary, Adani Renewable Energy Holding Twelve Ltd. The project, awarded by Uttar Pradesh Power Corporation Ltd (UPPCL), will supply power to the state for 25 years at a tariff of Rs 2.57 per kilowatt-hour (kWh).

AGEL posted an 85 percent jump in consolidated net profit to Rs 474 crore for the December quarter, up from Rs 256 crore a year ago, driven by higher power supply revenue. Revenue from power sales rose to Rs 1,993 crore from Rs 1,765 crore in the same period last year.

Read more: Trump optimistic about US-India trade talks, calls PM Modi a ‘smart man’

The company’s operational capacity expanded by 37 percent year-on-year to 11,609 MW, with the addition of 3,131 MW of new power plants. AGEL accounted for 15 percent of India’s utility-scale solar installations and 12 percent of wind capacity additions in calendar year 2024.

Shares of the company ended 1.24 percent lower at Rs 948 in the previous session but have gained 22.4 percent over the past month. However, the Adani Green Energy stock has been trading over 50 percent lower in the last six months.Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. SEN. JOHN KENNEDY (R-LA)

JEANINE PIRRO, FOX NEWS HOST

JEANINE PIRRO, MSNBC HOST: And joining me now is one of my favorite senators, Louisiana Senator John Kennedy.

All right, Senator, one of the most jarring things we learned is about how much fraud there was, particularly with Social Security. Take a listen.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

ARAM MOGHADDASSI, DOGE ENGINEER: At Social Security, one of the first things we learned is that they get phone calls every day of people trying to change direct deposit information. So when you want to change your bank account, you can call Social Security. We learned 40 percent of the phone calls that they get are from fraudsters.

BRET BAIER, FOX NEWS ANCHOR: 40 percent.

MOGHADDASSI: That's right, almost half.

MUSK: Yes, and they steal people's social security is what happens, is they call in, they say, they claim to be a retiree, then they — and they convince the Social Security person on the phone to change the — where the money is flowing. It actually goes to some fraudster. This is happening all day, every day. And then somebody doesn't receive their Social Security —

(END VIDEO CLIP)

PIRRO: You know, Senator, it's the way they've committed crimes for years. You just take another address and call them and tell them that you moved,

SEN. JOHN KENNEDY (R-LA): I think Judge that Mr. Musk and his team's efforts to control spending are the most important in the history of ever, certainly in my lifetime. My Democratic colleagues would prefer that we ask, who needs to pay more in taxes? The American people want us to ask, what the hell happened to the money? Since 2019, America's population is up 2 percent, spending is up 55 percent. President Biden's favorite type of spending was more.

This led to inflation and debt. But it wasn't just the quality. It was the quality. As Mr. Musk is showing us, a lot of this money that we're spending, things that you're spending money on are just money sucks. They're spending porn. We'd be better off spending this money on scratch tickets and Blackjack, and we will never get prices down and control inflation until we deregulate the economy so goods and services will cost less until we design a tax code that looks like somebody designed it on purpose and until we control spending. That's just Econ 101.

PIRRO: I agree, but Senator, I hope that Elon is listening tonight, because we have a suggestion on the next DOGE cut. Biden's broadband plan. Take a listen.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

SEN. JOHN THUNE (R-SD): More than three years ago, Congress created the $42.5 billion BEAD (ph) program to bring broadband services to unserved parts of the country. But to-date, it has not connected a single household to the internet. The Biden administration chose to add a host of conditions to the funding that made it unworkable for many providers.

(END VIDEO CLIP)

PIRRO: And you know, Senator, it kind of reminds me of all those electric vehicle charging stations. How many billions went into that? And they were like, none up.

KENNEDY: Well, that's why I voted against the Inflation Reduction Act. It appropriated $42 billion to give internet to people who don't have it. We haven't given it to anybody. We need to start over. The Department of Commerce needs to throw out the old rule, and we need to start over.

But let me make one other point, Judge. I want to make a general suggestion to Mr. Musk and the President. Number one, I think part of the problem is that Mr. Musk has not talked enough other than on Twitter. And his team have not talked enough about what they've been doing and why they're doing it. They need to hold a press conference every two weeks for the press and the American people that say this is what we've done, this is what we found, this is how we did it.

Number two, I begged the President last week, I'm going to keep begging. When Mr. Musk gets rid of waste, he's sued and the judges enjoying it, the only way for us to get rid of the spending porn permanently is for the President to send us over what's called a rescission bill.

PIRRO: Right.

KENNEDY: Pick 10 or 20 of the worst evidences or the worst items of waste and say, Congress, I want you to get rid of it and let us get rid of it and make the Democrats vote on it.

PIRRO: Well, you know, it sounds — it sounds like a great idea, and I agree with you, Senator, because so many people thought the DOGE team was a bunch of kids in hoodies in a basement somewhere, a bunch of thugs. They're billionaires businessmen who are putting their lives on hold, who are willing to answer all the questions, who have a website set up. I mean, everything is on the up and up, and the only thing the Democrats can say is that somebody may lose a Social Security check when they don't have a fact to add but let me read this. Senator, they tell me, I have to wrap with you. Thanks so much. It's great having you on the show. Take care.

END

SEN. CHRIS VAN HOLLEN (D-MD) IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC`S

DEADLINE: WHITE HOUSE

MARCH 28, 2025

SPEAKERS:

SEN. CHRIS VAN HOLLEN (D-MD)

ALICIA MENENDEZ, MSNBC HOST

ALICIA MENENDEZ, MSNBC HOST: Joining us now, one of the Democrats that signed the letter we just mentioned, Maryland Senator Chris Van Hollen. Senator, thank you for being with us. Let's talk about that video that we all just watched, the tactics ICE agents use on Ozturk, and then for the next 24 hours, her location being unknown even to her own lawyers.

SEN. CHRIS VAN HOLLEN (D-MD): Well, Alicia, you're absolutely right. Look, this is a terrifying video. It was obviously terrifying for her, Ms. Ozturk. It's also terrifying for our country —

MENENDEZ: Uh-hmm.

VAN HOLLEN: — that you could have these agents, unidentified, swoop in, sweep her off the streets, and essentially disappear her to Louisiana, apparently, against a court order for exercising First Amendment rights. This should not be happening in America. This is the kind of secret police operation you would expect to see in authoritarian regimes.

MENENDEZ: Well, in — in addition to the fact that they weren't clear about where she was going, they have yet, they being DHS, to publicly specify the alleged activities that led to her arrest. And that is a pattern, senator, as you know, is emerging. What does the lack of transparency on the part of DHS tell you about their intentions?

VAN HOLLEN: Well, it tells us that they have no legal basis that they want to at least present to the public because everyone will see just how flimsy it is. Because what appears to be happening is they're snatching these individuals based on their exercise of free speech rights. Right? Because of their pro-Palestinian advocacy. And in our country, you can exercise your right to controversial speech, and the government is not allowed to punish you for it. There's no crime alleged in any of these cases.

It appears to be based simply on what these individuals said, and that is illegal because the First Amendment does protect individuals who are here residing in the United States.

MENENDEZ: Well, let's talk about that because "The New York Times" notes that some of the tactics that we are watching, they're not particularly new, but what is shifting are the targets, immigrants with valid visas and legal status. The fact that they are testing these theories, senator, on folks who have one step down from American citizenship, they do have a series of legal protections in this country, and they are not being granted due process.

What should that tell us about their intention of applying some of the same concepts, ideas to American citizens?

VAN HOLLEN: Right. As you often see in the cases of authoritarian regimes, you know, they begin by focusing on the weakest links, people who they think have less rights and less protections, as a first step for coming for everybody else. And that's why it's so important that we take it seriously, not only to protect the rights of these individuals, but because this is a slippery slope.

And I think the Trump administration clearly is testing the limits of their powers. I think they very clearly overstepped their legal powers in these cases. I hope judges will rein them in because these are — these are core issues. Now, they are trying to dig up McCarthy era statutes in some cases and really stretch their meaning beyond any plausible application.

MENENDEZ: It's interesting. I am — I am looking as we came on air. We got this ruling that is going to pause her — her deportation. One, the fact that the case law they are referencing is now Mahmoud Khalil tells you just how quickly this is all happening. The fact that it is falling on these judges to — to be the ones to pump the brakes and actually maintain some semblance of justice in this country.

What happens if in cases like this, as we've seen in the case of Venezuelan nationals who were deported to El Salvador, this administration chooses to defy court orders?

VAN HOLLEN: Well, that's exactly right. I mean, when they go over that tripwire, it's going to be very important for two things to happen. One is judges need to exercise their power to sanction individuals who defy court orders. I know that judges don't want to take that step, but they really have to.

And then it is going to be important for members of Congress on a bipartisan basis to stand up and protect the rule of law. Now, I have not seen Republicans doing that to date. Right? A lot of them will quietly express concerns about what's happening, but they're still too afraid of a — of a statement or tweet from Donald Trump or Elon Musk.

And — and that is terrifying because if they can't stand up or won't stand up to protect the law and the Constitution, we are in a very deep, deep hole. We're already there, but we are going to dig ourselves deeper if people don't stand up. It's why — it's why I've said today that we have to applaud and stand up for the law firms and the universities and colleges that are standing up to Trump's bullying.

And we have to — we have to say that those that are caving in are making a huge mistake. It's a short road to tyranny when people start caving into Donald Trump's bullying, illegal bullying.

MENENDEZ: I think sometimes when you talk about the memos, you talk about going after law firms, which I fully agree is — is huge and critical and is where we began this show. That can seem so amorphous. And I think part of what makes this story so powerful is that when you see video of government agents showing up on a street and whisking a single individual away and not — and then not being transparent about why they did it, about where it is they are taking her to, there is just something much more immediate about the response.

And I wonder, in light of the surfacing of this video, if you have Republican colleagues who have laid eyes on it, who also — I know that they don't want to go out and say this in public, but have expressed any reservation to you about the way in which this administration is acting with impunity.

VAN HOLLEN: So, the short answer is some, privately. I think next week, I'm going to directly approach some of my Republican Senate colleagues who have said that they are champions of the First Amendment to work together to make a public statement about this.

And we'll see what happens because you can't claim to be for the First Amendment, and then run for cover as soon as it's violated by the Trump administration, which is exactly what's happening. So, this is one of those moments, Alicia, and I do intend to test at least some of our Republican colleagues who say they're champions of the first amendment and free speech.

MENENDEZ: All right. Senator Chris Van Hollen, next week, we will be eyes on you to see how those conversations go. Thank you for taking the time.

END
